Let me plan the migration carefully:

## Pages Router → App Router migration mapping

### Pages to migrate:
1. `pages/index.js` → `app/page.tsx` (SSR → Server Component with dynamic rendering)
2. `pages/_app.js` → `app/layout.tsx` (App wrapper with context provider)
3. `pages/_document.js` → `app/layout.tsx` (metadata, fonts, HTML shell)
4. `pages/_error.js` → `app/error.tsx` (Error boundary)
5. `pages/404.js` → `app/not-found.tsx` (404 page)
6. `pages/blog/index.js` → `app/blog/page.tsx` (Blog index with ISR)
7. `pages/blog/[id].js` → `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` (Dynamic blog post with ISR)
8. `pages/api/posts/index.js` → `app/api/posts/route.ts` (Route handler)
9. `pages/api/posts/[id].js` → `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` (Route handler)

### Key App Router mappings:
- `getServerSideProps` → Server Component with `fetch` (dynamic rendering, no `getServerSideProps` needed)
- `getStaticProps` → Server Component with `fetch` and `next: { revalidate }`
- `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams` + `dynamicParams: 'blocking'` (or just `export const dynamicParams = true`)
- `getInitialProps` → Error boundary component pattern
- `next/head` → `metadata` export + `next/head` still works but with differences
- `pages/api` → `app/api/.../route.ts` with `NextRequest`/`NextResponse`
- `_app.js` → `app/layout.tsx` (root layout)
- `_document.js` → Settings in `layout.tsx` via metadata
- `_error.js` → `app/error.tsx`
- `404.js` → `app/not-found.tsx`

You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7 8 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9 export default function Page({ 10 params, 11 searchParams, 12 }: { 13 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15 }) { 16 return

17 } 18 ``` 19 20 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22 return

My Page

23 } 24 ``` 25 26 ## Good to know 27 28 - The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29 - A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30 - A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31 - Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32 33 ## Reference 34 35 ### Props 36 37 #### `params` (optional) 38 39 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 40 41 ```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 42 export default async function Page({ 43 params, 44 }: { 45 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 46 }) { 47 const { slug } = await params 48 } 49 ``` 50 51 ```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 52 export default async function Page({ params }) { 53 const { slug } = await params 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 | Example Route | URL | `params` | 58 | ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 59 | `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 60 | `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 61 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 62 63 - Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 64 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 65 66 #### `searchParams` (optional) 67 68 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 69 70 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 71 export default async function Page({ 72 searchParams, 73 }: { 74 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 75 }) { 76 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 ```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 81 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 82 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 83 } 84 ``` 85 86 Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 87 88 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 89 'use client' 90 import { use } from 'react' 91 92 export default function Page({ 93 searchParams, 94 }: { 95 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 96 }) { 97 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 98 } 99 ``` 100 101 ```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 102 'use client' 103 import { use } from 'react' 104 105 export default function Page({ searchParams }) { 106 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 107 } 108 ``` 109 110 | Example URL | `searchParams` | 111 | --------------- | ----------------------------- | 112 | `/shop?a=1` | `Promise<{ a: '1' }>` | 113 | `/shop?a=1&b=2` | `Promise<{ a: '1', b: '2' }>` | 114 | `/shop?a=1&a=2` | `Promise<{ a: ['1', '2'] }>` | 115 116 - Since the `searchParams` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 117 - In version 14 and earlier, `searchParams` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 118 - `searchParams` is a **[Dynamic API](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering)** whose values cannot be known ahead of time. It is used to define the `` and `` tags and other globally shared UI. 25 26 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 27 export default function RootLayout({ 28 children, 29 }: { 30 children: React.ReactNode 31 }) { 32 return ( 33 34 {children} 35 36 ) 37 } 38 ``` 39 40 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 41 export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 42 return ( 43 44 {children} 45 46 ) 47 } 48 ``` 49 50 ## Reference 51 52 ### Props 53 54 #### `children` (required) 55 56 Layout components should accept and use a `children` prop. During rendering, `children` will be populated with the route segments the layout is wrapping. These will primarily be the component of a child [Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) (if it exists) or [Page](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), but could also be other special files like [Loading](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) or [Error](/docs/app/getting-started/error-handling) when applicable. 57 58 #### `params` (optional) 59 60 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) object from the root segment down to that layout. 61 62 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.tsx" switcher 63 export default async function Layout({ 64 children, 65 params, 66 }: { 67 children: React.ReactNode 68 params: Promise<{ team: string }> 69 }) { 70 const { team } = await params 71 } 72 ``` 73 74 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js" switcher 75 export default async function Layout({ children, params }) { 76 const { team } = await params 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 | Example Route | URL | `params` | 81 | --------------------------------- | -------------- | ---------------------------------- | 82 | `app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js` | `/dashboard/1` | `Promise<{ team: '1' }>` | 83 | `app/shop/[tag]/[item]/layout.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ tag: '1', item: '2' }>` | 84 | `app/blog/[...slug]/layout.js` | `/blog/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 85 86 - Since the `params` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 87 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 88 89 ### Layout Props Helper 90 91 You can type layouts with `LayoutProps` to get a strongly typed `params` and named slots inferred from your directory structure. `LayoutProps` is a globally available helper. 92 93 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" 94 export default function Layout(props: LayoutProps<'/dashboard'>) { 95 return ( 96
97 {props.children} 98 {/* If you have app/dashboard/@analytics, it appears as a typed slot: */} 99 {/* {props.analytics} */} 100
101 ) 102 } 103 ``` 104 105 > **Good to know**: 106 > 107 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 108 > - After type generation, the `LayoutProps` helper is globally available. It doesn't need to be imported. 109 110 ### Root Layout 111 112 The `app` directory **must** include a **root layout**, which is the top-most layout in the root `app` directory. Typically, the root layout is `app/layout.js`. 113 114 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 115 export default function RootLayout({ 116 children, 117 }: { 118 children: React.ReactNode 119 }) { 120 return ( 121 122 {children} 123 124 ) 125 } 126 ``` 127 128 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 129 export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 130 return ( 131 132 {children} 133 134 ) 135 } 136 ``` 137 138 - The root layout **must** define `` and `` tags. 139 - You should **not** manually add `` tags such as `` and `<meta>` to root layouts. Instead, you should use the [Metadata API](/docs/app/getting-started/metadata-and-og-images) which automatically handles advanced requirements such as streaming and de-duplicating `<head>` elements. 140 - You can create **multiple root layouts**. Any layout without a `layout.js` above it is a root layout. Two common approaches: 141 - Using [route groups](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-groups) like `app/(shop)/layout.js` and `app/(marketing)/layout.js` 142 - Omitting `app/layout.js` so layouts in subdirectories like `app/dashboard/layout.js` and `app/blog/layout.js` each become root layouts for their respective directories. 143 - Navigating **across multiple root layouts** will cause a **full page load** (as opposed to a client-side navigation). 144 - The root layout can be under a **dynamic segment**, for example when implementing [internationalization](/docs/app/guides/internationalization) with `app/[lang]/layout.js`. 145 146 ## Caveats 147 148 ### Request Object 149 150 Layouts are cached in the client during navigation to avoid unnecessary server requests. 151 152 [Layouts](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) do not rerender. They can be cached and reused to avoid unnecessary computation when navigating between pages. By restricting layouts from accessing the raw request, Next.js can prevent the execution of potentially slow or expensive user code within the layout, which could negatively impact performance. 153 154 To access the request object, you can use [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) and [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) APIs in [Server Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) and Functions. 155 156 ```tsx filename="app/shop/layout.tsx" switcher 157 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 158 159 export default async function Layout({ children }) { 160 const cookieStore = await cookies() 161 const theme = cookieStore.get('theme') 162 return '...' 163 } 164 ``` 165 166 ```jsx filename="app/shop/layout.js" switcher 167 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 168 169 export default async function Layout({ children }) { 170 const cookieStore = await cookies() 171 const theme = cookieStore.get('theme') 172 return '...' 173 } 174 ``` 175 176 ### Query params 177 178 Layouts do not rerender on navigation, so they cannot access search params which would otherwise become stale. 179 180 To access updated query parameters, you can use the Page [`searchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#searchparams-optional) prop, or read them inside a Client Component using the [`useSearchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params) hook. Since Client Components re-render on navigation, they have access to the latest query parameters. 181 182 ```tsx filename="app/ui/search.tsx" switcher 183 'use client' 184 185 import { useSearchParams } from 'next/navigation' 186 187 export default function Search() { 188 const searchParams = useSearchParams() 189 190 const search = searchParams.get('search') 191 192 return '...' 193 } 194 ``` 195 196 ```jsx filename="app/ui/search.js" switcher 197 'use client' 198 199 import { useSearchParams } from 'next/navigation' 200 201 export default function Search() { 202 const searchParams = useSearchParams() 203 204 const search = searchParams.get('search') 205 206 return '...' 207 } 208 ``` 209 210 ```tsx filename="app/shop/layout.tsx" switcher 211 import Search from '@/app/ui/search' 212 213 export default function Layout({ children }) { 214 return ( 215 <> 216 <Search /> 217 {children} 218 </> 219 ) 220 } 221 ``` 222 223 ```jsx filename="app/shop/layout.js" switcher 224 import Search from '@/app/ui/search' 225 226 export default function Layout({ children }) { 227 return ( 228 <> 229 <Search /> 230 {children} 231 </> 232 ) 233 } 234 ``` 235 236 ### Pathname 237 238 Layouts do not re-render on navigation, so they do not access pathname which would otherwise become stale. 239 240 To access the current pathname, you can read it inside a Client Component using the [`usePathname`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-pathname) hook. Since Client Components re-render during navigation, they have access to the latest pathname. 241 242 ```tsx filename="app/ui/breadcrumbs.tsx" switcher 243 'use client' 244 245 import { usePathname } from 'next/navigation' 246 247 // Simplified breadcrumbs logic 248 export default function Breadcrumbs() { 249 const pathname = usePathname() 250 const segments = pathname.split('/') 251 252 return ( 253 <nav> 254 {segments.map((segment, index) => ( 255 <span key={index}> 256 {' > '} 257 {segment} 258 </span> 259 ))} 260 </nav> 261 ) 262 } 263 ``` 264 265 ```jsx filename="app/ui/breadcrumbs.js" switcher 266 'use client' 267 268 import { usePathname } from 'next/navigation' 269 270 // Simplified breadcrumbs logic 271 export default function Breadcrumbs() { 272 const pathname = usePathname() 273 const segments = pathname.split('/') 274 275 return ( 276 <nav> 277 {segments.map((segment, index) => ( 278 <span key={index}> 279 {' > '} 280 {segment} 281 </span> 282 ))} 283 </nav> 284 ) 285 } 286 ``` 287 288 ```tsx filename="app/docs/layout.tsx" switcher 289 import { Breadcrumbs } from '@/app/ui/Breadcrumbs' 290 291 export default function Layout({ children }) { 292 return ( 293 <> 294 <Breadcrumbs /> 295 <main>{children}</main> 296 </> 297 ) 298 } 299 ``` 300 301 ```jsx filename="app/docs/layout.js" switcher 302 import { Breadcrumbs } from '@/app/ui/Breadcrumbs' 303 304 export default function Layout({ children }) { 305 return ( 306 <> 307 <Breadcrumbs /> 308 <main>{children}</main> 309 </> 310 ) 311 } 312 ``` 313 314 ### Fetching Data 315 316 Layouts cannot pass data to their `children`. However, you can fetch the same data in a route more than once, and use React [`cache`](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) to dedupe the requests without affecting performance. 317 318 Alternatively, when using [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch)in Next.js, requests are automatically deduped. 319 320 ```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher 321 export async function getUser(id: string) { 322 const res = await fetch(`https://.../users/${id}`) 323 return res.json() 324 } 325 ``` 326 327 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" switcher 328 import { getUser } from '@/app/lib/data' 329 import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 330 331 export default async function Layout({ children }) { 332 const user = await getUser('1') 333 334 return ( 335 <> 336 <nav> 337 {/* ... */} 338 <UserName user={user.name} /> 339 </nav> 340 {children} 341 </> 342 ) 343 } 344 ``` 345 346 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.js" switcher 347 import { getUser } from '@/app/lib/data' 348 import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 349 350 export default async function Layout({ children }) { 351 const user = await getUser('1') 352 353 return ( 354 <> 355 <nav> '@/app/lib/data' 367 import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 368 369 export default async function Page() { 370 const user = await getUser('1') 371 372 return ( 373 <div> 374 <h1>Welcome {user.name}</h1> 375 </div> 376 ) 377 } 378 ``` 379 380 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/page.js" switcher 381 import { getUser } from '@/app/lib/data' 382 import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 383 384 export default async function Page() { 385 const user = await getUser('1') 386 387 return ( 388 <div> 389 <h1>Welcome {user.name}</h1> 390 </div> 391 ) 392 } 393 ``` 394 395 ### Accessing child segments 396 397 Layouts do not have access to the route segments below itself. To access all route segments, you can use [`useSelectedLayoutSegment`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-selected-layout-segment) or [`useSelectedLayoutSegments`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-selected-layout-segments) in a Client Component. 398 399 ```tsx filename="app/ui/nav-link.tsx" switcher 400 'use client' 401 402 import Link from 'next/link' 403 import { useSelectedLayoutSegment } from 'next/navigation' 404 405 export default function NavLink({ 406 slug, 407 children, 408 }: { 409 slug: string 410 children: React.ReactNode 411 }) { 412 const segment = useSelectedLayoutSegment() 413 const isActive = slug === segment 414 415 return ( 416 <Link 417 href={`/blog/${slug}`} 418 // Change style depending on whether the link is active 419 style={{ fontWeight: isActive ? 'bold' : 'normal' }} 420 > 421 {children} 422 </Link> 423 ) 424 } 425 ``` 426 427 ```jsx filename="app/ui/nav-link.js" switcher 428 'use client' 429 430 import Link from 'next/link' 431 import { useSelectedLayoutSegment } from 'next/navigation' 432 433 export default function NavLinks({ slug, children }) { 434 const segment = useSelectedLayoutSegment() 435 const isActive = slug === segment 436 437 return ( 438 <Link 439 href={`/blog/${slug}`} 440 style={{ fontWeight: isActive ? 'bold' : 'normal' }} 441 > 442 {children} 443 </Link> 444 ) 445 } 446 ``` 447 448 ```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 449 import { NavLink } from './nav-link' 450 import getPosts from './get-posts' 451 452 export default async function Layout({ 453 children, 454 }: { 455 children: React.ReactNode 456 }) { 457 const featuredPosts = await getPosts() 458 return ( 459 <div> 460 {featuredPosts.map((post) => ( 461 <div key={post.id}> 462 <NavLink slug={post.slug}>{post.title}</NavLink> 463 </div> 464 ))} 465 <div>{children}</div> 466 </div> 467 ) 468 } 469 ``` 470 471 ```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 472 import { NavLink } from './nav-link' 473 import getPosts from './get-posts' 474 475 export default async function Layout({ children }) { 476 const featuredPosts = await getPosts() 477 return ( 478 <div> 479 {featuredPosts.map((post) => ( 480 <div key={post.id}> 481 <NavLink slug={post.slug}>{post.title}</NavLink> 482 </div> 483 ))} 484 <div>{children}</div> 485 </div> 486 ) 487 } 488 ``` 489 490 ## Examples 491 492 ### Metadata 493 494 You can modify the `<head>` HTML elements such as `title` and `meta` using the [`metadata` object](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#the-metadata-object) or [`generateMetadata` function](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#generatemetadata-function). 495 496 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 497 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 498 499 export const metadata: Metadata = { 500 title: 'Next.js', 501 } 502 503 export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 504 return '...' 505 } 506 ``` 507 508 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 509 export const metadata = { 510 title: 'Next.js', 511 } 512 513 export default function Layout({ children }) { 514 return '...' 515 } 516 ``` 517 518 > **Good to know**: You should **not** manually add `<head>` tags such as `<title>` and `<meta>` to root layouts. 1 --- 2 title: route.js 3 description: API reference for the route.js special file. 4 --- 5 6 Route Handlers allow you to create custom request handlers for a given route using the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs. 7 8 ```ts filename="route.ts" switcher 9 export async function GET() { 10 return Response.json({ message: 'Hello World' }) 11 } 12 ``` 13 14 ```js filename="route.js" switcher 15 export async function GET() { 16 return Response.json({ message: 'Hello World' }) 17 } 18 ``` 19 20 ## Reference 21 22 ### HTTP Methods 23 24 A **route** file allows you to create custom request handlers for a given route. The following [HTTP methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods) are supported: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS`. 25 26 ```ts filename="route.ts" switcher 27 export async function GET(request: Request) {} 28 29 export async function HEAD(request: Request) {} 30 31 export async function POST(request: Request) {} 32 33 export async function PUT(request: Request) {} 34 35 export async function DELETE(request: Request) {} 36 37 export async function PATCH(request: Request) {} 38 39 // If `OPTIONS` is not defined, Next.js will automatically implement `OPTIONS` and set the appropriate Response `Allow` header depending on the other methods defined in the Route Handler. 40 export async function OPTIONS(request: Request) {} 41 ``` 42 43 ```js filename="route.js" switcher 44 export async function GET(request) {} 45 46 export async function HEAD(request) {} 47 48 export async function POST(request) {} 49 50 export async function PUT(request) {} 51 52 export async function DELETE(request) {} 53 54 export async function PATCH(request) {} 55 56 // If `OPTIONS` is not defined, Next.js will automatically implement `OPTIONS` and set the appropriate Response `Allow` header depending on the other methods defined in the Route Handler. 57 export async function OPTIONS(request) {} 58 ``` 59 60 ### Parameters 61 62 #### `request` (optional) 63 64 The `request` object is a [NextRequest](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request) object, which is an extension of the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) API. `NextRequest` gives you further control over the incoming request, including easily accessing `cookies` and an extended, parsed, URL object `nextUrl`. 65 66 ```ts filename="route.ts" switcher 67 import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server' 68 69 export async function GET(request: NextRequest) { 70 const url = request.nextUrl 71 } 72 ``` 73 74 ```js filename="route.js" switcher 75 export async function GET(request) { 76 const url = request.nextUrl 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 #### `context` (optional) 81 82 - **`params`**: a promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) for the current route. 83 84 ```ts filename="app/dashboard/[team]/route.ts" switcher 85 export async function GET( 86 request: Request, 87 { params }: { params: Promise<{ team: string }> } 88 ) { 89 const { team } = await params 90 } 91 ``` 92 93 ```js filename="app/dashboard/[team]/route.js" switcher 94 export async function GET(request, { params }) { 95 const { team } = await params 96 } 97 ``` 98 99 | Example | URL | `params` | 100 | -------------------------------- | -------------- | ---------------------------------- | 101 | `app/dashboard/[team]/route.js` | `/dashboard/1` | `Promise<{ team: '1' }>` | 102 | `app/shop/[tag]/[item]/route.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ tag: '1', item: '2' }>` | 103 | `app/blog/[...slug]/route.js` | `/blog/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 104 105 ### Route Context Helper 106 107 You can type the Route Handler context using `RouteContext` to get strongly typed `params` from a route literal. `RouteContext` is a globally available helper. 108 109 ```ts filename="app/users/[id]/route.ts" 110 import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server' 111 112 export async function GET(_req: NextRequest, ctx: RouteContext<'/users/[id]'>) { 113 const { id } = await ctx.params 114 return Response.json({ id }) 115 } 116 ``` 117 118 > **Good to know** 119 > 120 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 121 > - After type generation, the `RouteContext` helper is globally available. It doesn't need to be imported. 122 123 ## Examples 124 125 ### Cookies 126 127 You can read or set cookies with [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) from `next/headers`. 128 129 ```ts filename="route.ts" switcher 130 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 131 132 export async function GET(request: NextRequest) { 133 const cookieStore = await cookies() 134 135 const a = cookieStore.get('a') 136 const b = cookieStore.set('b', '1') 137 const c = cookieStore.delete('c') 138 } 139 ``` 140 141 ```js filename="route.js" switcher 142 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 143 144 export async function GET(request) { 145 const cookieStore = await cookies() 146 147 const a = cookieStore.get('a') 148 const b = cookieStore.set('b', '1') 149 const c = cookieStore.delete('c') 150 } 151 ``` 152 153 Alternatively, you can return a new `Response` using the [`Set-Cookie`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Set-Cookie) header. 154 155 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 156 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 157 158 export async function GET(request: Request) { 159 const cookieStore = await cookies() 160 const token = cookieStore.get('token') 161 162 return new Response('Hello, Next.js!', { 163 status: 200, 164 headers: { 'Set-Cookie': `token=${token.value}` }, 165 }) 166 } 167 ``` 168 169 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 170 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 171 172 export async function GET(request) { 173 const cookieStore = await cookies() 174 const token = cookieStore.get('token') 175 176 return new Response('Hello, Next.js!', { 177 status: 200, 178 headers: { 'Set-Cookie': `token=${token.value}` }, 179 }) 180 } 181 ``` 182 183 You can also use the underlying Web APIs to read cookies from the request ([`NextRequest`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request)): 184 185 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 186 import { type NextRequest } from 'next/server' 187 188 export async function GET(request: NextRequest) { 189 const token = request.cookies.get('token') 190 } 191 ``` 192 193 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 194 export async function GET(request) { 195 const token = request.cookies.get('token') 196 } 197 ``` 198 199 ### Headers 200 201 You can read headers with [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) from `next/headers`. 202 203 ```ts filename="route.ts" switcher 204 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 205 import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server' 206 207 export async function GET(request: NextRequest) { 208 const headersList = await headers() 209 const referer = headersList.get('referer') 210 } 211 ``` 212 213 ```js filename="route.js" switcher 214 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 215 216 export async function GET(request) { 217 const headersList = await headers() 218 const referer = headersList.get('referer') 219 } 220 ``` 221 222 This `headers` instance is read-only. GET(request: Request) { 228 const headersList = await headers() 229 const referer = headersList.get('referer') 230 231 return new Response('Hello, Next.js!', { 232 status: 200, 233 headers: { referer: referer }, 234 }) 235 } 236 ``` 237 238 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 239 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 240 241 export async function GET(request) { 242 const headersList = await headers() 243 const referer = headersList.get('referer') 244 245 return new Response('Hello, Next.js!', { 246 status: 200, 247 headers: { referer: referer }, 248 }) 249 } 250 ``` 251 252 You can also use the underlying Web APIs to read headers from the request ([`NextRequest`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request)): 253 254 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 255 import { type NextRequest } from 'next/server' 256 257 export async function GET(request: NextRequest) { 258 const requestHeaders = new Headers(request.headers) 259 } 260 ``` 261 262 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 263 export async function GET(request) { 264 const requestHeaders = new Headers(request.headers) 265 } 266 ``` 267 268 ### Revalidating Cached Data 269 270 You can = await data.json() 289 290 return Response.json(posts) 291 } 292 ``` 293 294 ### Redirects 295 296 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 297 import { redirect } from 'next/navigation' 298 299 export async function GET(request: Request) { 300 redirect('https://nextjs.org/') 301 } 302 ``` 303 304 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 305 import { redirect } from 'next/navigation' 306 307 export async function GET(request) { 308 redirect('https://nextjs.org/') 309 } 310 ``` 311 312 ### Dynamic Route Segments 313 314 Route Handlers can use [Dynamic Segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) to create request handlers from dynamic data. 315 316 ```ts filename="app/items/[slug]/route.ts" switcher 317 export async function GET( 318 request: Request, 319 { params }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> } 320 ) { 321 const { slug } = await params // 'a', 'b', or 'c' 322 } 323 ``` 324 325 ```js filename="app/items/[slug]/route.js" switcher 326 export async function GET(request, { params }) { 327 const { slug } = await params // 'a', 'b', or 'c' 328 } 329 ``` 330 331 | Route | Example URL | `params` | 332 | --------------------------- | ----------- | ------------------------ | 333 | `app/items/[slug]/route.js` | `/items/a` | `Promise<{ slug: 'a' }>` | 334 | `app/items/[slug]/route.js` | `/items/b` | `Promise<{ slug: 'b' }>` | 335 | `app/items/[slug]/route.js` | `/items/c` | `Promise<{ slug: 'c' }>` | 336 337 #### Static Generation with `generateStaticParams` 338 339 You can use [`generateStaticParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params) with dynamic Route Handlers to statically generate responses at build time for specified params, while handling other params dynamically at request time. 340 341 When using [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components), you can combine `generateStaticParams` with `use cache` to enable data caching for both prerendered and runtime params. 342 343 See the [generateStaticParams with Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params#with-route-handlers) documentation for examples and details. 344 345 ### URL Query Parameters 346 347 The request object passed to the Route Handler is a `NextRequest` instance, which includes [some additional convenience methods](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request#nexturl), such as those for more easily handling query parameters. 348 349 ```ts filename="app/api/search/route.ts" switcher 350 import { type NextRequest } from 'next/server' 351 352 export function GET(request: NextRequest) { 353 const searchParams = request.nextUrl.searchParams 354 const query = searchParams.get('query') 355 // query is "hello" for /api/search?query=hello 356 } 357 ``` 358 359 ```js filename="app/api/search/route.js" switcher 360 export function GET(request) { 361 const searchParams = request.nextUrl.searchParams 362 const query = searchParams.get('query') 363 // query is "hello" for /api/search?query=hello 364 } 365 ``` 366 367 ### Streaming 368 369 Streaming is commonly used in combination with Large Language Models (LLMs), such as OpenAI, for AI-generated content. Learn more about the [AI SDK](https://sdk.vercel.ai/docs/introduction). 370 371 ```ts filename="app/api/chat/route.ts" switcher 372 import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai' 373 import { StreamingTextResponse, streamText } from 'ai' 374 375 export async function POST(req: Request) { 376 const { messages } = await req.json() 377 const result = await streamText({ 378 model: openai('gpt-4-turbo'), 379 messages, 380 }) 381 382 return new StreamingTextResponse(result.toAIStream()) 383 } 384 ``` 385 386 ```js filename="app/api/chat/route.js" switcher 387 import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai' 388 import { StreamingTextResponse, streamText } from 'ai' 389 390 export async function POST(req) { 391 const { messages } = await req.json() 392 const result = await streamText({ 393 model: openai('gpt-4-turbo'), 394 messages, 395 }) 396 397 return new StreamingTextResponse(result.toAIStream()) 398 } 399 ``` 400 401 These abstractions use the Web APIs to create a stream. You can also use the underlying Web APIs directly. 402 403 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 404 // https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/ReadableStream#convert_async_iterator_to_stream 405 function iteratorToStream(iterator: any) { 406 return new ReadableStream({ 407 async pull(controller) { 408 const { value, done } = await iterator.next() 409 410 if (done) { 411 controller.close() 412 } else { 413 controller.enqueue(value) 414 } 415 }, 416 }) 417 } 418 419 function sleep(time: number) { 420 return new Promise((resolve) => { 421 setTimeout(resolve, time) 422 }) 423 } 424 425 const encoder = new TextEncoder() 426 427 async function* makeIterator() { 428 yield encoder.encode('

One

') 429 await sleep(200) 430 yield encoder.encode('

Two

') 431 await sleep(200) 432 yield encoder.encode('

Three

') 433 } 434 435 export async function GET() { 436 const iterator = makeIterator() 437 const stream = iteratorToStream(iterator) 438 439 return new Response(stream) 440 } 441 ``` 442 443 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 444 // https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/ReadableStream#convert_async_iterator_to_stream 445 function iteratorToStream(iterator) { 446 return new ReadableStream({ 447 async pull(controller) { 448 const { value, done } = await iterator.next() 449 450 if (done) { 451 controller.close() 452 } else { 453 controller.enqueue(value) 454 } 455 }, 456 }) 457 } 458 459 function sleep(time) { 460 return new Promise((resolve) => { 461 setTimeout(resolve, time) 462 }) 463 } 464 465 const encoder = new TextEncoder() 466 467 async function* makeIterator() { 468 yield encoder.encode('

One

') 469 await sleep(200) 470 yield encoder.encode('

Two

') 471 await sleep(200) 472 yield encoder.encode('

Three

') 473 } 474 475 export async function GET() { 476 const iterator = makeIterator() 477 const stream = iteratorToStream(iterator) 478 479 return new Response(stream) 480 } 481 ``` 482 483 ### Request Body 484 485 You can read the `Request` body using the standard Web API methods: 486 487 ```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher 488 export async function POST(request: Request) { 489 const res = await request.json() 490 return Response.json({ res }) 491 } 492 ``` 493 494 ```js filename="app/items/route.js" switcher 495 export async function POST(request) { 496 const res = await request.json() 497 return Response.json({ res }) 498 } 499 ``` 500 501 ### Request Body FormData 502 503 You can read the `FormData` using the `request.formData()` function: 504 505 ```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher 506 export async function POST(request: Request) { 507 const formData = await request.formData() 508 const name = formData.get('name') 509 const email = formData.get('email') 510 return Response.json({ name, email }) 511 } 512 ``` 513 514 ```js filename="app/items/route.js" switcher 515 export async function POST(request) { 516 const formData = await request.formData() 517 const name = formData.get('name') 518 const email = formData.get('email') 519 return Response.json({ name, email }) 520 } 521 ``` 522 523 Since `formData` data are all strings, you may want to use [`zod-form-data`](https://www.npmjs.com/zod-form-data) to validate the request and retrieve data in the format you prefer (e.g. `number`). 524 525 ### CORS 526 527 You can set CORS headers for a specific Route Handler using the standard Web API methods: 528 529 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 530 export async function GET(request: Request) { 531 return new Response('Hello, Next.js!', { 532 status: 200, 533 headers: { 534 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin': '*', 535 'Access-Control-Allow-Methods': 'GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS', 536 'Access-Control-Allow-Headers': 'Content-Type, Authorization', 537 }, 538 }) 539 } 540 ``` 541 542 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 543 export async function GET(request) { 544 return new Response('Hello, Next.js!', { 545 status: 200, 546 headers: { 547 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin': '*', 548 'Access-Control-Allow-Methods': 'GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS', 549 'Access-Control-Allow-Headers': 'Content-Type, Authorization', 550 }, 551 }) 552 } 553 ``` 554 555 > **Good to know**: 556 > 557 > - To add CORS headers to multiple Route Handlers, you can use [Proxy](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#cors) or the [`next.config.js` file](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/headers#cors). 558 559 ### Webhooks 560 561 You can use a Route Handler to receive webhooks from third-party services: 562 563 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 564 export async function POST(request: Request) { 565 try { 566 const text = await request.text() 567 // Process the webhook payload 568 } catch (error) { 569 return new Response(`Webhook error: ${error.message}`, { 570 status: 400, 571 }) 572 } 573 574 return new Response('Success!', { 575 status: 200, 576 }) 577 } 578 ``` 579 580 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 581 export async function POST(request) { 582 try { 583 const text = await request.text() 584 // Process the webhook payload 585 } catch (error) { 586 return new Response(`Webhook error: ${error.message}`, { 587 status: 400, 588 }) 589 } 590 591 return new Response('Success!', { 592 status: 200, 593 }) 594 } 595 ``` 596 597 Notably, unlike API Routes with the Pages Router, you do not need to use `bodyParser` to use any additional configuration. 598 599 ### Non-UI Responses 600 601 You can use Route Handlers to return non-UI content. Note that [`sitemap.xml`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap#generating-a-sitemap-using-code-js-ts), [`robots.txt`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots#generate-a-robots-file), [`app icons`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#generate-icons-using-code-js-ts-tsx), and [open graph images](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image) all have built-in support. 602 603 ```ts filename="app/rss.xml/route.ts" switcher 604 export async function GET() { 605 return new Response( 606 ` 607 608 609 610 Next.js Documentation 611 https://nextjs.org/docs 612 The React Framework for the Web 613 614 615 `, 616 { 617 headers: { 618 'Content-Type': 'text/xml', 619 }, 620 } 621 ) 622 } 623 ``` 624 625 ```js filename="app/rss.xml/route.js" switcher 626 export async function GET() { 627 return new Response(` 628 629 630 631 Next.js Documentation 632 https://nextjs.org/docs 633 The React Framework for the Web 634 635 636 `) 637 } 638 ``` 639 640 ### Segment Config Options 641 642 Route Handlers use the same [route segment configuration](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config) as pages and layouts. 643 644 ```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher 645 export const dynamic = 'auto' 646 export const dynamicParams = true 647 export const revalidate = false 648 export const fetchCache = 'auto' 649 export const runtime = 'nodejs' 650 export const preferredRegion = 'auto' 651 ``` 652 653 ```js filename="app/items/route.js" switcher 654 export const dynamic = 'auto' 655 export const dynamicParams = true 656 export const revalidate = false 657 export const fetchCache = 'auto' 658 export const runtime = 'nodejs' 659 export const preferredRegion = 'auto' 660 ``` 661 662 See the [API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config) for more details. 663 664 ## A [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#150) is available | 669 | `v15.0.0-RC` | The default caching for `GET` handlers was changed from static to dynamic | 670 | `v13.2.0` | Route Handlers are introduced. | 671

39

Something went wrong!

40 48
49 ) 50 } 51 ``` 52 53 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/error.js" switcher 54 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 55 56 import { useEffect } from 'react' 57 58 export default function Error({ error, reset }) { 59 useEffect(() => { 60 // Log the error to an error reporting service 61 console.error(error) 62 }, [error]) 63 64 return ( 65
66

Something went wrong!

67 75
76 ) 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 `error.js` wraps a route segment and its nested children in a [React Error Boundary](https://react.dev/reference/react/Component#catching-rendering-errors-with-an-error-boundary). When an error throws within the boundary, the `error` component shows as the fallback UI. 81 82 89 90 > **Good to know**: 91 > 92 > - The [React DevTools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools) allow you to toggle error boundaries to test error states. 93 > - If you want errors to bubble up to the parent error boundary, you can `throw` when rendering the `error` component. 94 95 ## Reference 96 97 ### Props 98 99 #### `error` 100 101 An instance of an [`Error`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Error) object forwarded to the `error.js` Client Component. 102 103 > **Good to know:** During development, the `Error` object forwarded to the client will be serialized and include the `message` of the original error for easier debugging. However, **this behavior is different in production** to avoid leaking potentially sensitive details included in the error to the client. 104 105 #### `error.message` 106 107 - Errors forwarded from Client Components show the original `Error` message. 108 - Errors forwarded from Server Components show a generic message with an identifier. This is to prevent leaking sensitive details. You can use the identifier, under `errors.digest`, to match the corresponding server-side logs. 109 110 #### `error.digest` 111 112 An automatically generated hash of the error thrown. It can be used to match the corresponding error in server-side logs. 113 114 #### `reset` 115 116 The cause of an error can sometimes be temporary. In these cases, trying again might resolve the issue. 117 118 An error component can use the `reset()` function to prompt the user to attempt to recover from the error. When executed, the function will try to re-render the error boundary's contents. If successful, the fallback error component is replaced with the result of the re-render. 119 120 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/error.tsx" switcher 121 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 122 123 export default function Error({ 124 error, 125 reset, 126 }: { 127 error: Error & { digest?: string } 128 reset: () => void 129 }) { 130 return ( 131
132

Something went wrong!

133 134
135 ) 136 } 137 ``` 138 139 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/error.js" switcher 140 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 141 142 export default function Error({ error, reset }) { 143 return ( 144
145

Something went wrong!

146 147
148 ) 149 } 150 ``` 151 152 ## Examples 153 154 ### Global Error 155 156 While less common, you can handle errors in the root layout or template using `global-error.jsx`, located in the root app directory, even when leveraging [internationalization](/docs/app/guides/internationalization). Global error UI must define its own `` and `` tags, global styles, fonts, or other dependencies that your error page requires. This file replaces the root layout or template when active. 157 158 > **Good to know**: Error boundaries must be [Client Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#using-client-components), which means that [`metadata` and `generateMetadata`](/docs/app/getting-started/metadata-and-og-images) exports are not supported in `global-error.jsx`. As an alternative, you can use the React [``](https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/components/title) component. 159 160 ```tsx filename="app/global-error.tsx" switcher 161 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 162 163 export default function GlobalError({ 164 error, 165 reset, 166 }: { 167 error: Error & { digest?: string } 168 reset: () => void 169 }) { 170 return ( 171 // global-error must include html and body tags 172 <html> 173 <body> 174 <h2>Something went wrong!</h2> 175 <button onClick={() => reset()}>Try again</button> 176 </body> 177 </html> 178 ) 179 } 180 ``` 181 182 ```jsx filename="app/global-error.js" switcher 183 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 184 185 export default function GlobalError({ error, reset }) { 186 return ( 187 // global-error must include html and body tags 188 <html> 189 <body> 190 <h2>Something went wrong!</h2> 191 <button onClick={() => reset()}>Try again</button> 192 </body> 193 </html> 194 ) 195 } 196 ``` 197 198 ### Graceful error recovery with a custom error boundary 199 200 When rendering fails on the client, it can be useful to show the last known server rendered UI for a better user experience. 201 202 The `GracefullyDegradingErrorBoundary` is an example of a custom error boundary that captures and preserves the current HTML before an error occurs. If a rendering error happens, it re-renders the captured HTML and displays a persistent notification bar to inform the user. 203 204 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/error.tsx" switcher 205 'use client' 206 207 import React, { Component, ErrorInfo, ReactNode } from 'react' 208 209 interface ErrorBoundaryProps { 210 children: ReactNode 211 onError?: (error: Error, errorInfo: ErrorInfo) => void 212 } 213 214 interface ErrorBoundaryState { 215 hasError: boolean 216 } 217 218 export class GracefullyDegradingErrorBoundary extends Component< 219 ErrorBoundaryProps, 220 ErrorBoundaryState 221 > { 222 private contentRef: React.RefObject<HTMLDivElement | null> 223 224 constructor(props: ErrorBoundaryProps) { 225 super(props) 226 this.state = { hasError: false } 227 this.contentRef = React.createRef() 228 } 229 230 static getDerivedStateFromError(_: Error): ErrorBoundaryState { 231 return { hasError: true } 232 } 233 234 componentDidCatch(error: Error, errorInfo: ErrorInfo) { 235 if (this.props.onError) { 236 this.props.onError(error, errorInfo) 237 } 238 } 239 240 render() { 241 if (this.state.hasError) { 242 // Render the current HTML content without hydration 243 return ( 244 <> 245 <div 246 ref={this.contentRef} 247 suppressHydrationWarning 248 dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ 249 __html: this.contentRef.current?.innerHTML || '', 250 }} 251 /> 252 <div className="fixed bottom-0 left-0 right-0 bg-red-600 text-white py-4 px-6 text-center"> 253 <p className="font-semibold"> 254 An error occurred during page rendering 255 </p> 256 </div> 257 </> 258 ) 259 } 260 261 return <div ref={this.contentRef}>{this.props.children}</div> 262 } 263 } 264 265 export default GracefullyDegradingErrorBoundary 266 ``` 267 268 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/error.js" switcher 269 'use client' 270 271 import React, { Component, createRef } from 'react' 272 273 class GracefullyDegradingErrorBoundary extends Component { 274 constructor(props) { 275 super(props) 276 this.state = { hasError: false } 277 this.contentRef = createRef() 278 } 279 280 static getDerivedStateFromError(_) { 281 return { hasError: true } 282 } 283 284 componentDidCatch(error, errorInfo) { 285 if (this.props.onError) { 286 this.props.onError(error, errorInfo) 287 } 288 } 289 290 render() { 291 if (this.state.hasError) { 292 // Render the current HTML content without hydration 293 return ( 294 <> 295 <div 296 ref={this.contentRef} 297 suppressHydrationWarning 298 dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ 299 __html: this.contentRef.current?.innerHTML || '', 300 }} 301 /> 302 <div className="fixed bottom-0 left-0 right-0 bg-red-600 text-white py-4 px-6 text-center"> 303 <p className="font-semibold"> 304 An error occurred during page rendering 305 </p> 306 </div> 307 </> 308 ) 309 } 310 311 return <div ref={this.contentRef}>{this.props.children}</div> 312 } 313 } 314 315 export default GracefullyDegradingErrorBoundary 316 ``` 317 318 ## Version History 319 320 | Version | Changes | 321 | --------- | 1 --- 2 title: not-found.js 3 description: API reference for the not-found.js file. 4 --- 5 6 Next.js provides two conventions to handle not found cases: 7 8 - **`not-found.js`**: Used when you call the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function in a route segment. 9 - **`global-not-found.js`**: Used to define a global 404 page for unmatched routes across your entire app. This is handled at the routing level and doesn't depend on rendering a layout or page. 10 11 ## `not-found.js` 12 13 The **not-found** file is used to render UI when the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function is thrown within a route segment. Along with serving a custom UI, Next.js will return a `200` HTTP status code for streamed responses, and `404` for non-streamed responses. 14 15 ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher 16 import Link from 'next/link' 17 18 export default function NotFound() { 19 return ( 20
21

Not Found

22

Could not find requested resource

23 Return Home 24
25 ) 26 } 27 ``` 28 29 ```jsx filename="app/blog/not-found.js" switcher 30 import Link from 'next/link' 31 32 export default function NotFound() { 33 return ( 34
35

Not Found

36

Could not find requested resource

37 Return Home 38
39 ) 40 } 41 ``` 42 43 ## `global-not-found.js` (experimental) 44 45 The `global-not-found.js` file lets you define a 404 page for your entire application. Unlike `not-found.js`, which works at the route level, this is used when a requested URL doesn't match any route at all. Next.js **skips rendering** and directly returns this global page. 46 47 The `global-not-found.js` file bypasses your app's normal rendering, which means you'll need to import any global styles, fonts, or other dependencies that your 404 page requires. 48 49 > **Good to know**: A smaller version of your global styles, and a simpler font family could improve performance of this page. 50 51 `global-not-found.js` is useful when you can't build a 404 page using a combination of `layout.js` and `not-found.js`. This can happen in two cases: 52 53 - Your app has multiple root layouts (e.g. `app/(admin)/layout.tsx` and `app/(shop)/layout.tsx`), so there's no single layout to compose a global 404 from. 54 - Your root layout is defined using top-level dynamic segments (e.g. `app/[country]/layout.tsx`), which makes composing a consistent 404 page harder. 55 56 To enable it, add the `globalNotFound` flag in `next.config.ts`: 57 58 ```tsx filename="next.config.ts" 59 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 60 61 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 62 experimental: { 63 globalNotFound: true, 64 }, 65 } 66 67 export default nextConfig 68 ``` 69 70 Then, create a file in the root of the `app` directory: `app/global-not-found.js`: 71 72 ```tsx filename="app/global-not-found.tsx" switcher 73 // Import global styles and fonts 74 import './globals.css' 75 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' 76 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 77 78 const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) 79 80 export const metadata: Metadata = { 81 title: '404 - Page Not Found', 82 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 83 } 84 85 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 86 return ( 87 88 89

404 - Page Not Found

90

This page does not exist.

91 92 93 ) 94 } 95 ``` 96 97 ```jsx filename="app/global-not-found.js" switcher 98 // Import global styles and fonts 99 import './globals.css' 100 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' 101 102 const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) 103 104 export const metadata = { 105 title: '404 - Page Not Found', 106 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 107 } 108 109 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 110 return ( 111 112 113

404 - Page Not Found

114

This page does not exist.

115 116 117 ) 118 } 119 ``` 120 121 Unlike `not-found.js`, this file must return a full HTML document, including `` and `` tags. 122 123 ## Reference 124 125 ### Props 126 127 `not-found.js` or `global-not-found.js` components do not accept any props. 128 129 > **Good to know**: In addition to catching expected `notFound()` errors, the root `app/not-found.js` and `app/global-not-found.js` files handle any unmatched URLs for your whole application. This means users that visit a URL that is not handled by your app will be shown the exported UI. 130 131 ## Examples 132 133 ### Data Fetching 134 135 By default, `not-found` is a Server Component. You can mark it as `async` to fetch and display data: 136 137 ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher 138 import Link from 'next/link' 139 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 140 141 export default async function NotFound() { 142 const headersList = await headers() 143 const domain = headersList.get('host') 144 const data = await getSiteData(domain) 145 return ( 146
147

Not Found: {data.name}

153 ) 154 } 155 ``` 156 157 ```jsx filename="app/not-found.jsx" switcher 158 import Link from 'next/link' 159 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 160 161 export default async function NotFound() { 162 const headersList = await headers() 163 const domain = headersList.get('host') 164 const data = await getSiteData(domain) 165 return ( 166
167

Not Found: {data.name}

1 --- 2 title: loading.js 3 description: API reference for the loading.js file. 4 --- 5 6 The special file `loading.js` helps you create meaningful Loading UI with [React Suspense](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense). With this convention, you can show an [instant loading state](#instant-loading-states) from the server while the content of a route segment streams in. The new content is automatically swapped in once complete. 7 8 15 16 ```tsx filename="app/feed/loading.tsx" switcher 17 export default function Loading() { 18 // Or a custom loading skeleton component 19 return

Loading...

20 } 21 ``` 22 23 ```jsx filename="app/feed/loading.js" switcher 24 export default function Loading() { 25 // Or a custom loading skeleton component 26 return

Loading...

27 } 28 ``` 29 30 Inside the `loading.js` file, you can add any light-weight loading UI. You may find it helpful to use the [React Developer Tools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools) to manually toggle Suspense boundaries. 31 32 By default, this file is a [Server Component](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) - but can also be used as a Client Component through the `"use client"` directive. 33 34 ## Reference 35 36 ### Parameters 37 38 Loading UI components do not accept any parameters. 39 40 ## Behavior 41 42 ### Navigation 43 44 - The Fallback UI is [prefetched](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching), making navigation immediate unless prefetching hasn't completed. 45 - Navigation is interruptible, meaning changing routes does not need to wait for the content of the route to fully load before navigating to another route. 46 - Shared layouts remain interactive while new route segments load. 47 48 ### Instant Loading States 49 50 An instant loading state is fallback UI that is shown immediately upon navigation. You can pre-render loading indicators such as skeletons and spinners, or a small but meaningful part of future screens such as a cover photo, title, etc. This helps users understand the app is responding and provides a better user experience. 51 52 Create a loading state by adding a `loading.js` file inside a folder. 53 54 61 62 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/loading.tsx" switcher 63 export default function Loading() { 64 // You can add any UI inside Loading, including a Skeleton. 65 return 66 } 67 ``` 68 69 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/loading.js" switcher 70 export default function Loading() { 71 // You can add any UI inside Loading, including a Skeleton. 72 return 73 } 74 ``` 75 76 In the same folder, `loading.js` will be nested inside `layout.js`. It will automatically wrap the `page.js` file and any children below in a `` boundary. 77 78 85 86 ### SEO 87 88 - For bots that only scrape static HTML, and cannot execute JavaScript like a full browser, such as Twitterbot, Next.js resolves [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) before streaming UI, and metadata is placed in the `` of the initial HTML. 89 - Otherwise, [streaming metadata](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#streaming-metadata) may be used. Next.js automatically detects user agents to choose between blocking and streaming behavior. 90 - Since streaming is server-rendered, it does not impact SEO. You can use the [Rich Results Test](https://search.google.com/test/rich-results) tool from Google to see how your page appears to Google's web crawlers and view the serialized HTML ([source](https://web.dev/rendering-on-the-web/#seo-considerations)). 91 92 ### Status Codes 93 94 When streaming, a `200` status code will be returned to signal that the request was successful. 95 96 The server can still communicate errors or issues to the client within the streamed content itself, for example, when using [`redirect`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/redirect) or [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found). Because the response headers have already been sent to the client, the status code of the response cannot be updated. 97 98 For example, when a 404 page is streamed to the client, Next.js includes a `` tag in the streamed HTML. This prevents search engines from indexing that URL even if the HTTP status is 200. See Google’s guidance on the [`robots` meta tag](https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag). 99 100 Some crawlers may label these responses as “soft 404s”. In the streaming case, this does not lead to indexation because the page is explicitly marked `noindex` in the HTML. 101 102 If you need a 404 status, for compliance or analytics, ensure the resource exists before the response body is streamed, so that the server can set the HTTP status code. 103 104 You can run this check in [`proxy`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy) to rewrite missing slugs to a not-found route, or [produce a 404 response](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy#producing-a-response). Keep proxy checks fast, and avoid fetching full content there. 105 106
107 When is the response body streamed? 108 109 The response body starts streaming when a Suspense fallback renders (for example, a `loading.tsx`) or when a Server Component suspends under a `Suspense` boundary. Place `notFound()` before those boundaries and before any `await` that may suspend. 110 111 To start streaming, the response headers must be set. This is why it is not possible to change the status code after streaming started. 112 113
114 115 ### Browser limits 116 117 [Some browsers](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=252413) buffer a streaming response. You may not see the streamed response until the response exceeds 1024 bytes. This typically only affects “hello world” applications, but not real applications. 118 119 ## Platform Support 120 121 | Deployment Option | Supported | 122 | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------- | 123 | [Node.js server](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#nodejs-server) | Yes | 124 | [Docker container](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#docker) | Yes | 125 | [Static export](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#static-export) | No | 126 | [Adapters](/docs/app/getting-started/deploying#adapters) | Platform-specific | 127 128 Learn how to [configure streaming](/docs/app/guides/self-hosting#streaming-and-suspense) when self-hosting Next.js. 129 130 ## Examples 131 132 ### Streaming with Suspense 133 134 In addition to `loading.js`, you can also manually create Suspense Boundaries for your own UI components. The App Router supports streaming with [Suspense](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense). 135 136 `` works by wrapping a component that performs an asynchronous action (e.g. fetch data), showing fallback UI (e.g. skeleton, spinner) while it's happening, and then swapping in your component once the action completes. 137 138 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/page.tsx" switcher 139 import { Suspense } from 'react' 140 import { PostFeed, Weather } from './Components' 141 142 export default function Posts() { 143 return ( 144
145 Loading feed...

}> 146 147 148 Loading weather...

}> 149 150 151
152 ) 153 } 154 ``` 155 156 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/page.js" switcher 157 import { Suspense } from 'react' 158 import { PostFeed, Weather } from './Components' 159 160 export default function Posts() { 161 return ( 162
163 Loading feed...

}> 164 165 166 Loading weather...

}> 167 168 169
170 ) 171 } 172 ``` 173 174 By using Suspense, you get the benefits of: 175 176 1. **Streaming Server Rendering** - Progressively rendering HTML from the server to the client. 177 2. **Selective Hydration** - React prioritizes what components to make interactive first based on user interaction. 178 179 For more Suspense examples and use cases, please see the [React Documentation](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense). 180 181 ## Version History 182 183 | Version | Changes | 184 | --------- | --------------------- | 185 | `v13.0.0` | `loading` introduced. | 186

For example, a blog could include the following route `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` where `[slug]` is the Dynamic Segment for blog posts. 17 18 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 19 export default async function Page({ 20 params, 21 }: { 22 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 23 }) { 24 const { slug } = await params 25 return

### Catch-all Segments 85 86 Dynamic Segments can be extended to **catch-all** subsequent segments by adding an ellipsis inside the brackets `[...folderName]`. 87 88 For example, `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` will match `/shop/clothes`, but also `/shop/clothes/tops`, `/shop/clothes/tops/t-shirts`, and so on. 89 90 | Route | Example URL | `params` | 91 | ---------------------------- | ------------- | --------------------------- | 92 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a` | `{ slug: ['a'] }` | 93 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b'] }` | 94 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b/c` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b', 'c'] }` | 95 96 ### Optional Catch-all Segments 97 98 Catch-all Segments can be made **optional** by including the parameter in double square brackets: `[[...folderName]]`. 99 100 For example, `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` will **also** match `/shop`, in addition to `/shop/clothes`, `/shop/clothes/tops`, `/shop/clothes/tops/t-shirts`. 101 102 The difference between **catch-all** and **optional catch-all** segments is that with optional, the route without the parameter is also matched (`/shop` in the example above). 103 104 | Route | Example URL | `params` | 105 | ------------------------------ | ------------- | --------------------------- | 106 | `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop` | `{ slug: undefined }` | 107 | `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop/a` | `{ slug: ['a'] }` | 108 | `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b'] }` | 109 | `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b/c` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b', 'c'] }` | 110 111 ### TypeScript 112 113 When using TypeScript, you can add types for `params` depending on your configured route segment — use [`PageProps<'/route'>`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper), [`LayoutProps<'/route'>`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper), or [`RouteContext<'/route'>`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) to type `params` in `page`, `layout`, and `route` respectively. 114 115 Route `params` values are typed as `string`, `string[]`, or `undefined` (for optional catch-all segments), because their values aren't known until runtime. Users can enter any URL into the address bar, and these broad types help ensure that your application code handles all these possible cases. 116 117 | Route | `params` Type Definition | 118 | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | 119 | `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `{ slug: string }` | 120 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `{ slug: string[] }` | 121 | `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `{ slug?: string[] }` | 122 | `app/[categoryId]/[itemId]/page.js` | `{ categoryId: string, itemId: string }` | 123 124 If you're working on a route where `params` can only have a fixed number of valid values, such as a `[locale]` param with a known set of language codes, you can use runtime validation to handle any invalid params a user may enter, and let the rest of your application work with the narrower type from your known set. 125 126 ```tsx filename="/app/[locale]/page.tsx" 127 import { notFound } from 'next/navigation' 128 import type { Locale } from '@i18n/types' 129 import { isValidLocale } from '@i18n/utils' 130 131 function assertValidLocale(value: string): asserts value is Locale { 132 if (!isValidLocale(value)) notFound() 133 } 134 135 export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/[locale]'>) { 136 const { locale } = await props.params // locale is typed as string 137 assertValidLocale(locale) 138 // locale is now typed as Locale 139 } 140 ``` 141 142 ## Behavior 143 144 - Since the `params` prop is a promise. You must use `async`/`await` or React's use function to access the values. 145 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 146 147 ### With Cache Components 148 149 When using [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) with dynamic route segments, how you handle params depends on whether you use [`generateStaticParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params). 150 151 Without `generateStaticParams`, param values are unknown during prerendering, making params runtime data. You must wrap param access in `` boundaries to provide fallback UI. 152 153 With `generateStaticParams`, you provide sample param values that can be used at build time. The build process validates that dynamic content and other runtime APIs are correctly handled, then generates static HTML files for the samples. Pages rendered with runtime params are saved to disk after a successful first request. 154 155 The sections below demonstrate both patterns. 156 157 #### Without `generateStaticParams` 158 159 All params are runtime data. Param access must be wrapped by Suspense fallback UI. Next.js generates a static shell at build time, and content loads on each request. 160 161 > **Good to know**: You can also use [`loading.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) for page-level fallback UI. 162 163 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 164 import { Suspense } from 'react' 165 166 export default function Page({ params }: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 167 return ( 168

You can prerender all routes or a subset depending on your needs. 195 196 During the build process, the route is executed with each sample param to collect the HTML result. If dynamic content or runtime data are accessed incorrectly, the build will fail. 197 198 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" highlight={3-5,8,19} 199 import { Suspense } from 'react' 200 201 export async function generateStaticParams() { 202 return [{ slug: '1' }, { slug: '2' }, { slug: '3' }] 203 } 204 205 export default async function Page({ params }: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 206 const { slug } = await params 207 208 return ( 209

If your route has conditional logic that accesses runtime APIs for certain param values not in your samples, those branches won't be validated at build time: 234 235 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 236 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 237 238 export async function generateStaticParams() { 239 return [{ slug: 'public-post' }, { slug: 'hello-world' }] 240 } 241 242 export default async function Page({ params }: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 243 const { slug } = await params 244 245 if (slug.startsWith('private-')) { 246 // This branch is never executed at build time 247 // Runtime requests for 'private-*' slugs will error 248 return 249 } 250 251 return 252 } 253 254 async function PrivatePost({ slug }: { slug: string }) { 255 const token = (await cookies()).get('token') 256 // ... fetch and render private post using token for auth 257 } 258 ``` 259 260 For runtime params not returned by `generateStaticParams`, validation occurs during the first request. In the example above, requests for slugs starting with `private-` will fail because `PrivatePost` accesses `cookies()` without a Suspense boundary. Other runtime params that don't hit the conditional branch will render successfully and be saved to disk for subsequent requests. 261 262 To fix this, wrap `PrivatePost` with Suspense: 263 264 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" highlight={13-15} 265 import { Suspense } from 'react' 266 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 267 268 export async function generateStaticParams() { 269 return [{ slug: 'public-post' }, { slug: 'hello-world' }] 270 } 271 272 export default async function Page({ params }: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 273 const { slug } = await params 274 275 if (slug.startsWith('private-')) { 276 return ( 277 Loading...}> 278 279 280 ) 281 } 282 283 return 284 } 285 286 async function PrivatePost({ slug }: { slug: string }) { 287 const token = (await cookies()).get('token') 288 // ... fetch and render private post using token for auth 289 } 290 ``` 291 292 ## Examples 293 294 ### With `generateStaticParams` 295 296 The [`generateStaticParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params) function can be used to [statically generate](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes at build time instead of on-demand at request time. 297 298 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 299 export async function generateStaticParams() { 300 const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json()) 301 302 return posts.map((post) => ({ 303 slug: post.slug, 304 })) 305 } 306 ``` 307 308 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 309 export async function generateStaticParams() { 310 const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json()) 311 312 return posts.map((post) => ({ 313 slug: post.slug, 314 })) 315 } 316 ``` 317 318 When using `fetch` inside the `generateStaticParams` function, the requests are [automatically deduplicated](/docs/app/guides/caching#request-memoization). This avoids multiple network calls for the same data Layouts, Pages, and other `generateStaticParams` functions, speeding up build time. 319 320 ### Dynamic GET Route Handlers with `generateStaticParams` 321 322 `generateStaticParams` also works with dynamic [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) to statically generate API responses at build time: 323 324 ```ts filename="app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts" switcher 325 export async function generateStaticParams() { 326 const posts: { id: number }[] = await fetch( 327 'https://api.vercel.app/blog' 328 ).then((res) => res.json()) 329 330 return posts.map((post) => ({ 331 id: `${post.id}`, 332 })) 333 } 334 335 export async function GET( 336 request: Request, 337 { params }: RouteContext<'/api/posts/[id]'> 338 ) { 339 const { id } = await params 340 const res = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${id}`) 341 342 if (!res.ok) { 343 return Response.json({ error: 'Post not found' }, { status: 404 }) 344 } 345 346 const post = await res.json() 347 return Response.json(post) 348 } 349 ``` 350 351 ```js filename="app/api/posts/[id]/route.js" switcher 352 export async function generateStaticParams() { 353 const posts = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog').then((res) => 354 res.json() 355 ) 356 357 return posts.map((post) => ({ 358 id: `${post.id}`, 359 })) 360 } 361 362 export async function GET(request, { params }) { 363 const { id } = await params 364 const res = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${id}`) 365 366 if (!res.ok) { 367 return Response.json({ error: 'Post not found' }, { status: 404 }) 368 } 369 370 const post = await res.json() 371 return Response.json(post) 372 } 373 ``` 374 375 In this example, route handlers for all blog post IDs returned by `generateStaticParams` will be statically generated at build time. Requests to other IDs will be handled dynamically at request time. 376

`_document.js` had: HTML shell, fonts, meta tags - In App Router: Root layout defines ``, ``, metadata, and wraps children 2. **`app/page.tsx`** - Home page (replaces `pages/index.js`) - `getServerSideProps` → Server Component with direct `fetch` (dynamic rendering) - No `getServerSideProps` needed - just make the component `async` and fetch directly - Use `headers()` from `next/headers` instead of `req.headers['user-agent']` - Use `next/navigation` instead of `next/router` - Use `Link` instead of `router.push` for navigation 3. **`app/blog/page.tsx`** - Blog index (replaces `pages/blog/index.js`) - `getStaticProps` with `revalidate: 60` → Server Component with `fetch` and `revalidate` export - `router.push('/blog/${post.id}')` → `Link` component 4. **`app/blog/[id]/page.tsx`** - Blog post detail (replaces `pages/blog/[id].js`) - `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` with `revalidate: 300` → Server Component with fetch and `revalidate` export - `fallback: 'blocking'` → `export const dynamicParams = true` - `router.isFallback` → Not needed with `dynamicParams`, but we can show a loading state - `router.back()` → `Link` with `router.back()` via `'use client'` or `useRouter` in client 5. **`app/not-found.tsx`** - 404 page (replaces `pages/404.js`) - `app/not-found.tsx` handles unmatched routes - `useRouter` from `next/router` → `useRouter` from `next/navigation` 6. **`app/error.tsx`** - Error boundary (replaces `pages/_error.js`) - Must be a Client Component (`'use client'`) - `Error.getInitialProps` → `error` prop with `digest` and `reset()` function 7. **`app/global-error.tsx`** - Global error (needed since we're replacing `_error.js` which catches everything) 8. **`app/api/posts/route.ts`** - Posts API (replaces `pages/api/posts/index.js`) - Export named HTTP method functions instead of a single `handler` 9. **`app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts`** - Post detail API (replaces `pages/api/posts/[id].js`) - Export named HTTP method functions 10. **`app/blog/loading.tsx`** - Loading state for the blog pages ### The `next/head` → metadata approach: - Pages Router used `next/head` with `` component - App Router uses `metadata` export or `generateMetadata` function - The `Head` component from `next/head` works but metadata exports are preferred ### `next/router` → `next/navigation`: - `useRouter` from `next/router` → `useRouter` from `next/navigation` - The new `useRouter` has `push()`, `back()`, etc. ### `pages/api` → `app/api/.../route.ts`: - Named exports: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE` - Request/Response Web API objects - Dynamic params are a Promise Let me also check one more thing - about how revalidation works in App Router pages. For blog pages with ISR (revalidate), in App Router: - You can use `export const revalidate = 60` at the route segment level - Or use `next: { revalidate: 60 }` in the fetch call - Since we're using `fetch` directly, we can use `next: { revalidate: 60 }` option For the home page with SSR (`getServerSideProps`): - In App Router, server components are async by default - We can use `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` or just fetch in the component - Since we're reading `headers()`, it automatically opts into dynamic rendering Let me plan the file migrations: ### Home page (`app/page.tsx`): - Remove `getServerSideProps` - use Server Component with async data fetching - Use `headers()` from `next/headers` instead of `req.headers` - Use `Link` from `next/link` instead of `router.push` - Navigate to blog with Link instead of button click ### Blog index (`app/blog/page.tsx`): - Remove `getStaticProps` - use async component with fetch - Use `export const revalidate = 60` for ISR - Replace button + router.push with `Link` ### Blog post (`app/blog/[id]/page.tsx`): - `generateStaticParams` instead of `getStaticPaths` - `export const dynamicParams = true` instead of `fallback: 'blocking'` - `export const revalidate = 300` for ISR - `params` is a Promise - needs await - For `router.back()`, use `useRouter` from `next/navigation` in a client component, or a Link Wait, the blog post page uses `router.isFallback` and `router.back()`. In App Router: - `isFallback` isn't a concept - with `dynamicParams = true`, ungenerated routes render on demand - For `router.back()`, we can use a client component wrapper or just a `Link` component Let me think about this more carefully. The blog post has: 1. `router.isFallback` check - In App Router, with `dynamicParams = true`, the page renders on first request. There's no "fallback" state in the same way. If the page is loading, the `` boundary from `loading.tsx` handles it. 2. `router.back()` - This needs a client component. I can either use `button` with `onClick={() => window.history.back()}` or extract a small client component. Actually, looking at this more carefully, since the page uses `params` which is a Promise, the component is already `async`, so it's a server component. The `router.back()` needs client functionality. I can make a small client component for the back button. My Blog

 ) } ``` Wait, the docs say "You should not manually add `` tags such as `` and `<meta>` to root layouts. Instead, you should use the Metadata API." So I should not put `<link>` and `<meta>` in the `<head>` in the layout. Instead, I should: - Use `metadata` export for meta tags - For fonts, use `next/font` or put link tags in the head element Actually, `<link>` tags like favicon and preconnect CAN go in the head of the layout. The warning is about `<title>` and `<meta>` specifically. Let me re-read... "You should **not** manually add `<head>` tags such as `<title>` and `<meta>` to root layouts. Instead, you should use the [Metadata API](/docs/app/getting-started/metadata-and-og-images) which automatically handles advanced requirements such as streaming and de-duplicating `<head>` elements." So for `<link>` tags, we can just put them in the `<head>` of the root layout. All Pages Router files have been migrated to the App Router and the build passes with zero errors. Here's what was done:

### Files Created (`app/`)

| App Router | Replaces | Key Changes |
|---|---|---|
| `app/layout.tsx` | `_app.js` + `_document.js` | Metadata API for `<head>`, `next/font/google` for Inter font, `AppProvider` wraps all pages |
| `app/page.tsx` | `pages/index.js` | `getServerSideProps` → Server Component with `headers()` + direct `fetch` |
| `app/blog/page.tsx` | `pages/blog/index.js` | `getStaticProps` → `export const revalidate = 60` + async component |
| `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` | `pages/blog/[id].js` | `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams`, `getStaticProps` → async component, `dynamicParams: true` instead of `fallback: 'blocking'` |
| `app/blog/loading.tsx` | — (new) | Suspense fallback for blog routes |
| `app/not-found.tsx` | `pages/404.js` | Server Component, `<Link>` navigation |
| `app/error.tsx` | `pages/_error.js` (partial) | Client Component error boundary with `error` + `reset` props |
| `app/global-error.tsx` | `pages/_error.js` (root) | Full `<html>` error boundary for root-level crashes |
| `app/api/posts/route.ts` | `pages/api/posts/index.js` | Named `GET`/`POST` exports with Web `Request`/`Response` API |
| `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` | `pages/api/posts/[id].js` | Named `GET`/`PUT`/`DELETE` exports, `params` is a `Promise` |

### API Pattern Replacements

| Pages Router | App Router |
|---|---|
| `getServerSideProps({ req })` | Server Component + `headers()` from `next/headers` |
| `getStaticProps` + `revalidate` | `export const revalidate = N` + async component |
| `getStaticPaths` + `fallback: 'blocking'` | `generateStaticParams` + `export const dynamicParams = true` |
| `getInitialProps` | `error.tsx` / `global-error.tsx` error boundary pattern |
| `next/head` `<Head>` | `metadata` / `generateMetadata` export |
| `next/router` `useRouter` | `next/navigation` `useRouter` (for client components) |
| `pages/api` single handler | Named HTTP method exports in `route.ts` |
| `res.status().json()` | `NextResponse.json()` or `Response.json()` |
| `_app.js` + `_document.js` | `app/layout.tsx` root layout |
